concept: Multi-provider CLI dispatch patterns for Octopus-style reviews (Codex, Gemini, Mistral)
content:
# Multi-provider CLI dispatch — operational rules

Captured during the IX R7+R1+R2+R3 session, 13 April 2026, after four-provider reviews of the ix-autograd crate.

## Codex CLI (`codex exec`)

**Rule 1 — The `rmcp::transport::async_rw: Error reading from stream: serde error EOF` line is a WARNING, not a failure.**
Codex prints it when stdin isn't a TTY and it can't speak MCP handshake. The actual response FOLLOWS the ERROR line. Route stderr to stdout (`2>&1`), then `sed -n '30,$p'` past the boilerplate to find the real content.

**Rule 2 — Close stdin explicitly with `</dev/null`.**
Without it, Codex hangs waiting for MCP input because it thinks it might be running as an MCP server.

**Rule 3 — Wait the full 90-180s.**
Codex takes 60-120s for a ~1500-word response. Checking at 30s shows an empty file and looks like a failure.

**Rule 4 — Windows CreateProcessWithLogonW 1056 is not fatal.**
Codex shelling out to PowerShell for Get-Content sometimes hits the WDAC sandbox. It recovers and falls back to its own file reading.

**Correct invocation:**
```bash
codex exec --full-auto "prompt text here" </dev/null > /tmp/out.txt 2>&1 &
PID=$!
for i in $(seq 1 18); do kill -0 $PID 2>/dev/null || break; sleep 10; done
wait $PID
sed -n '30,$p' /tmp/out.txt
```

**Strength:** highest-quality correctness analysis. Walks the algorithm and verifies shapes/dims/tolerances with concrete numeric reasoning. Best for "does the code do what it says?" reviews.

## Gemini CLI (`gemini -p "..." -o text --approval-mode yolo`)

**Rule 1 — The first ~40 lines are MCP extension loading noise.**
Skip past `Loading extension: ...`, `Registering notification handlers for server ...`, `MCP context refresh ...`, and `Coalescing burst refresh ...` lines. Real content starts after the last `MCP context refresh complete.`

**Rule 2 — `[MCP error]` on individual downstream servers is not fatal.**
Gemini continues past them.

**Rule 3 — Fast (~30s for 1500 words), good for ergonomics/design reviews.**
Less good at deep correctness than Codex.

**Strength:** lateral thinking, API ergonomics, edge-case enumeration. Best for "what could go wrong?" reviews.

## Mistral via REST API (`MISTRAL_API_KEY` + Python urllib)

Octopus does not have a first-class Mistral provider yet. Call `https://api.mistral.ai/v1/chat/completions` directly.

**Rule 1 — DO NOT use `codestral-latest` for reviews or long-form analysis.**
Documented degenerate failure mode: gets stuck generating number sequences (`5, 10, 15, ..., 2400`) and burns the entire `max_tokens` budget on useless output. Observed 13 April 2026 with `temperature=0.2`. Not rescued by lower temperature.

**Rule 2 — Use `mistral-large-latest` instead.**
Mistral Large 2 produces structured reviews competently. Cost slightly higher (~1900 completion tokens for an 8k prompt) but output is usable.

**Rule 3 — Fact-check Mistral Large's cited line numbers.**
~50% hallucination rate on line numbers and library APIs. Before acting on any Mistral finding, open the cited file:line and verify. Observed during the R7 Day 2 review: Mistral Large invented `Axis::keepdims()` (doesn't exist in ndarray 0.17), claimed panics in paths that explicitly return Err, and cited fabricated line numbers.

**Strength:** Rust-specific idioms, performance observations (clones, unnecessary allocations). Good fourth voice IF you fact-check. Cheaper than Claude Opus for bulk suggestion generation. EU-hosted (relevant for aerospace/EU data sovereignty).

## Four-provider dispatch pattern for high-stakes reviews

1. Codex → correctness, algorithm walk
2. Gemini → ergonomics, edge cases
3. Mistral Large → Rust idioms, perf
4. Claude → fact-check + synthesis

Costs ~$1 in API calls for a 1000-line review vs ~$6 Claude-only. Quality delta: second-opinion triangulation catches bugs any single model would miss. Time: ~90s parallel.

## Anti-pattern observed in this session

**Do NOT retry a failing CLI dispatch without reading the full output file.**
The third Codex retry in the Day 2 review was "free" because the first two had already produced full 1500-word responses that were hidden behind the `rmcp` ERROR header I was only head-checking. Always tail the output file (`sed -n '30,$p'` or `cat`) before concluding a dispatch failed.
